source: All-In Podcast — Markets: pricing in tariffs, Trump vs Powell, the $10 trillion AGI prize
summary: Trump's sensitivity to stock market performance acts as a dampening mechanism on tariff escalation
summary_extended: Baker argues that Trump's demonstrated sensitivity to stock market performance serves as a structural moderating force on tariff escalation, limiting the probability of the most extreme policy outcomes. He observes that after a brief period of apparent indifference, Trump returned to publicly citing all-time highs as a measure of success, which Baker interprets as evidence that market feedback loops remain intact and politically meaningful to the administration. This dynamic, Baker contends, creates a de facto ceiling on tariff severity because sufficiently damaging trade policy would trigger market declines that Trump would find politically unacceptable. The investment implication is that investors should not price in the most catastrophic tariff scenarios, as the market itself acts as a check on policy extremism, though Baker acknowledges meaningful uncertainty remains around near-term outcomes, particularly heading into August.
detail: Baker observes Trump returned to citing all-time highs after a brief period of indifference, suggesting market feedback will constrain the most extreme tariff scenarios. This is a structural moderating force on policy volatility.
confidence_evidence: Baker states this directly and with some conviction but acknowledges uncertainty about August outcomes
quote_fragment: he clearly cares and is clearly very sensitive to market feedback
